title: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: organoids, synthetic embryos, and off-the-shelf banks

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: organoids, synthetic embryos, and off-the-shelf banks
Let me end with where the field is going.
There are three frontiers I want to sketch, each one extending the iPSC concept further.
Frontier one: organoids.
If you take iPSCs and differentiate them in three-dimensional culture, allowing the cells to organize themselves spatially, they don't just become single cell types.
They build small, organized structures called organoids that contain multiple cell types arranged the way they would be in a real organ.
You can make brain organoids — small balls of neurons and glia organized into rudimentary cortical structures.
Intestinal organoids with villi and crypts.
Liver organoids.
Kidney organoids.
Lung organoids.
Each one is a partial, miniaturized version of a real organ, derived from a single iPSC line, grown in a dish.
Organoids are not real organs — they're missing vasculature, they're small, they lack the complex spatial cues of an embryo.
But they are far better tissue models than any single-cell-type culture.
You can study how cells interact, how diseases progress in a multi-cellular context, how drugs affect entire mini-organs.
Brain organoids have been used to model microcephaly caused by Zika virus infection, to study schizophrenia, to test psychiatric drugs.
Intestinal organoids are being used to model inflammatory bowel disease and to develop personalized therapies.
The organoid field has exploded in the past decade and is becoming its own major branch of biology.
Frontier two: synthetic embryos.
Even more provocatively, researchers have started using pluripotent stem cells — both ES cells and iPSCs — to build structures that resemble early embryos, without ever using a fertilized egg.

By combining cells representing the embryonic and the extra-embryonic lineages, in the right geometry and culture conditions, they can self-assemble into structures that look strikingly like early post-implantation embryos.
These are sometimes called embryoids, gastruloids, or stembryos.

In 2022 and 2023, several labs reported mouse synthetic embryos that developed in vitro to the equivalent of mid-gestation — with a beating heart, the start of neural tube, recognizable body plan organization — without any sperm or egg involvement, starting only from cultured stem cells.
The ethical and regulatory questions here are deep.
If you can build something embryo-like from stem cells, what is the moral status of that structure?
Human versions of this work are being pursued cautiously, with ongoing ethical debate about how long such structures should be allowed to develop.
Frontier three: off-the-shelf allogeneic iPSC banks.
I mentioned this earlier but I want to come back to it because it's likely to define the next decade of clinical iPSC use.
The vision is: build a bank of perhaps a few hundred well-characterized iPSC lines, each with a different HLA type, collectively covering most of the genetic diversity of the population.
Differentiate each line, at scale, into the major clinically useful cell types — cardiomyocytes, dopamine neurons, beta cells, hematopoietic progenitors, RPE, others.
Manufacture these cells under pharmaceutical-grade conditions, characterize them exhaustively, freeze them.
When a patient needs a cell therapy, you match them by HLA type to the best line in the bank, thaw the appropriate cells, and infuse.
Like a blood bank, but for tissue.
Japan, under Yamanaka's CiRA institute, has been building such a bank since the mid-2010s, with the explicit goal of covering most of the Japanese population's HLA diversity using a few dozen lines.
Other countries are following.

Companies like Fate Therapeutics, Century Therapeutics, and Cellino are building variations on this model — sometimes with HLA-engineered "universal" cells designed to be invisible to any patient's immune system.
If this works, iPSC therapy stops being a custom-manufactured boutique product and becomes a mass-producible pharmaceutical-grade tissue product.
Off-the-shelf cells, available in any hospital, matched to the patient like blood type.
That's the operational endpoint of everything Yamanaka started in 2006.
So that's where we are.
Twenty years from a single Cell paper to a field that spans developmental biology, regenerative medicine, drug screening, disease modeling, rejuvenation research, and synthetic embryology.
Yamanaka pressed rewind on a skin cell, and the entire field of cell biology pressed rewind alongside him.
Let me give you a final summing-up, and then we'll be done.
